coredata.dc:title: Platinum, palladium, and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots as efficient enhancers for acetonitrile-hydrogen peroxide chemiluminescence reaction and its application for sensitive determination of Fe(II) ions
coredata.dc:creator: Delnavaz, Elnaz
Amjadi, Mohammad
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we investigated the effect of platinum, palladium, and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (Pt,Pd,N-CDs) on the acetonitrile-hydrogen peroxide (CH3CN–H2O2) chemiluminescence (CL) reaction. We found that Pt,Pd,N-CDs can remarkably amplify the CL intensity of this system (about 530-fold). Based on the gathered proofs, we deduced that the generated (1O2)2* molecules from the interaction between CH3CN and H2O2 under the alkaline medium can transfer their energy to Pt,Pd,N-CDs, which act as final emitting species in this CL reaction. Furthermore, we observed that the synthesized CDs function as catalysts, increasing the number of reactive oxygen species. In the presence of Fe(II), the CL signal of Pt,Pd,N-CD-based the system is greatly enhanced, which can be imputed to Fe(II)-catalyzed H2O2 dissociation. Utilizing this finding, an efficient CL assay was developed for the determination of Fe(II) in the range of 0.002–8.0 µM with a detection limit of 1.0 nM. Ultimately, the practicality and reliability of this method were tested by analyzing a certified reference material and spiked water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Noble metal
Reactive oxygen species
Sensor
Metal ion-doped carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The role of Pt,Pd,N-CDs in the chemiluminescence reaction

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–vis absorption spectrum of Pt,Pd,N-CDs. Inset: Photograph of Pt,Pd,N-CDs under the excitation of UV light with 365 nm.

Time profile of (A) CH3CN–H2O2, (B) CH3CN–H2O2–N-CDs, (C) CH3CN–H2O2–Pd,N-CDs, (D) CH3CN–H2O2–Pt,N-CDs, and (E) CH3CN–H2O2–Pt,Pd,N-CDs systems. Conditions: CH3CN (40 v/v%), H2O2 (0.225 M), NaOH (0.2 M), N-CDs (100 μL), Pd,N-CDs (100 μL), Pt,N-CDs (100 μL), Pt,Pd,N-CDs (100 μL).
(a) CL spectrum of CH3CN–H2O2–Pt,Pd,N-CDs system recorded by the continuous flow of the reagents. (b) Fluorescence spectrum of Pt,Pd,N-CDs with λex = 440 nm.
Calibration curve and corresponding CL signals for Fe(II).
Influence of various radical acceptors on CH3CN–H2O2–Pt,Pd,N-CDs.
Results for the Fe(II) assay in real samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Mean of three determinations ± standard deviation.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Results for the Fe(II) assay in oyster tissue.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Mean of three determinations ± standard deviation.

head.ce:title: Platinum, palladium, and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots as efficient enhancers for acetonitrile-hydrogen peroxide chemiluminescence reaction and its application for sensitive determination of Fe(II) ions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz 5166616471, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz 5166616471, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we investigated the effect of platinum, palladium, and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (Pt,Pd,N-CDs) on the acetonitrile-hydrogen peroxide (CH3CN–H2O2) chemiluminescence (CL) reaction. We found that Pt,Pd,N-CDs can remarkably amplify the CL intensity of this system (about 530-fold). Based on the gathered proofs, we deduced that the generated (1O2)2* molecules from the interaction between CH3CN and H2O2 under the alkaline medium can transfer their energy to Pt,Pd,N-CDs, which act as final emitting species in this CL reaction. Furthermore, we observed that the synthesized CDs function as catalysts, increasing the number of reactive oxygen species. In the presence of Fe(II), the CL signal of Pt,Pd,N-CD-based the system is greatly enhanced, which can be imputed to Fe(II)-catalyzed H2O2 dissociation. Utilizing this finding, an efficient CL assay was developed for the determination of Fe(II) in the range of 0.002–8.0 µM with a detection limit of 1.0 nM. Ultimately, the practicality and reliability of this method were tested by analyzing a certified reference material and spiked water samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Iron, an abundant transition element, plays a crucial role in sustaining human health. The key function of this element in the human body is to aid in the generation of myoglobin, hemoglobin, and enzymes [1]. An iron deficiency can lead to various health issues such as anemia, kidney and liver diseases, cardiovascular diseases, and diabetes. In contrast, excessive amounts of it cause oxidation and damage to nucleic acids, lipids, and proteins [2]. Variable contents of iron exist in natural waters, depending on the geological location and other factors. Mineral and underground waters can be rich of the Fe2+ form of iron, while in surface water it is mainly in Fe3+ form. Therefore, accurate determination of Fe2+ ions is vital in environmental and food samples due to the significant health risks posed by its high levels. Several techniques have been developed for this purpose, such as colorimetric [3,4], fluorescence [5], chemiluminescence (CL) [1], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [6], electrochemistry [7], and flow injection method [8,9]. In the meantime, a few efforts have been concentrated on the design of the CL-based approaches for selective and sensitive detection of Fe2+. Recently, Shah et al. demonstrated that the ultra-weak CL from N-doped carbon dots/H2O2 system could be amplified by Fe2+ based on the Fenton reaction [1]. Therefore, here, we focused on the introduction of a facile and sensitive CL method for the analysis of Fe2+.

Carbon Dots (CDs) are a member of the carbon nanomaterials family, including graphene, fullerenes, and carbon nanotubes which have attract great attention in various fields (especially optical sensing) [10,11]. Contrary to the incomparable properties of CDs, the lack of functional groups on the surface and low quantum yield have limited their utilization in diverse fields [12]. Heteroatom doping and surface functionalization are beneficial approaches to modifying CDs. So far, some non-metallic elements, such as S [13], N [14], B [15], and P [16] have doped into CDs. Metal ions, especially transition metal ions, have more unoccupied orbitals and electrons compared to non-metals, which facilitates the altering of the charge density on the surface of CDs [17]. Also, doping metal ions improves the optical properties and the advent of catalytic characteristics in this class of quantum dots [18]. Recently, investigations have been accomplished on CDs doped with different metal ions such as Cu [17,19], Fe [20], Co [21], and Pd [22].

Here we describe the application of Pt,Pd,N-CDs in the CL system and demonstrate their considerable enhancing effect on CH3CN–H2O2 reaction. Also, we compared the efficiency of the corresponding single metal ion-doped CDs (Pt,N-CDs and Pd,N-CDs) on the CL signal. It was found that Pt,Pd,N-CDs strongly amplify the CL intensity under the alkaline medium, which demonstrates the synergetic effect of Pd and Pt ions doped CDs. The Pt,Pd,N-CDs-based CL reaction is then applied for sensitive determination of Fe(II) ions.

A LUMAT LB9507 instrument (Berthold, Germany) was used to record the CL signals. CL and fluorescence spectra were recorded applying the Jasco FP-8300 spectrofluorimeter (Japan). UV-Vis measurements were performed by a Cary-100 spectrophotometer (Varian). The characterization of the Pt,Pd,N-CDs was performed using X-ray diffraction (XRD, D5000, Siemens, USA, the radiation source was Cu Kα), FT-IR spectrometry (Bruker Co., Germany; www.bruker.com), EDX (MIRA III, France; www.tescan.com) spectroscopy, and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Leo 906, Zeiss, Germany; www.zeiss.com).

Chloroplatinic acid, Palladium chloride, diethylenetriamine (DETA), iron (II) sulfate hexahydrate, citric acid monohydrate, sodium hydroxide, hydrogen peroxide, and acetonitrile (99.8%) were bought from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany; www.merckmillipore.com). Deionized water (aqua MAX-Ultra 370, Young Lin Instrument Co., Korea) was utilized in all analysis. Hydrogen peroxide solution with a concentration of 3.0 M was prepared daily. Also, the stock solution of Fe(II) was prepared daily by dissolving a proper amount of iron(II) sulfate hexahydrate in deionized water.
Different types of CDs were synthesized according to the literature with small amendments. Synthesis methods are given in the electronic supplementary materials (ESM) in detail.

Initially, a certain volume of Fe(II) standard solution, 100 µL of Pt,Pd,N-CDs, 75 µL of H2O2 (3.0 M), and 400 µL of acetonitrile were poured into the CL cuvette. Then, the final volume was reached to 900 µL with deionized water. Afterward, the CL reaction was triggered by injecting 100 µL of NaOH (2.0 M). The maximum intensity in CL profile was employed as analytical signal.

Mineral and well water samples were obtained from the Vata Company (Ardabil, Iran) and the environment around Tabriz (Iran), respectively. Water samples were filtered via filter paper to segregate the particulate substances. Afterward, a relevant amount of these solutions was utilized for Fe(II) assay according to the mentioned procedure.

The wet ashing procedure was used to prepare oyster samples (NIST 1566b CRM). Oyster tissue powder (25.0 mg) was poured into a round bottom flask. Then, 0.8 mL of perchloric acid and 3 mL of HNO3 were appended to the flask and refluxed at 150 ◦C for 3 h. Afterward, the acidic mixture was dried at 200 ◦C. Pale yellow residue was dissolved in HNO3 (1%) and pH was regulated to 6.0 with NaOH for the next usage. To confirm the change of all iron content to Fe(II) in the standard sample, 500 µL of hydroxylamine (1.0 M) solution and 500 µL of acetate buffer (0.1 M) were added to the solution containing 1 mL of the standard solution. Finally, 200 µL of the obtained solution was analyzed according to the proposed method.

TEM image was recorded to investigate the morphology (shape, size) of the synthesized Pt,Pd,N-CDs. As Fig. 1 displays, the synthesized Pt,Pd,N-CDs are spherical and their size distribution is less than 10 nm. Fig. 2 illustrates FT-IR spectrum of Pt,Pd,N-CDs. The appeared peaks in 3108, 1734, 1386, and 1189 cm−1 are related to the O-H, CO, C-N, and C-O stretching vibrations, respectively. The peaks at 2612 and 784 cm−1 are provoked via C-H bonds. Also, the observed peaks at 629 and 590 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of Pd-O and Pt-O bonds, respectively [23,24].

The XRD pattern of the synthesized CDs is shown in Fig. S1. The diffraction pattern exhibits multiple sharp peaks in the range of 2θ=10–600, which indicates the formation of a well-ordered structure with high crystallinity. The sharp peak at 19.58° is imputed to (002) plane of carbon and 42.82◦ is ascribed to (101) plane, which also points to a high degree of graphitization of the CDs [25–30].
The EDX analysis graph (Fig. S2) demonstrates that the Pt,Pd,N-CDs are composed of C, N, O, Pt, and Pd elements.

Fluorescence and UV-Vis spectroscopy were used to investigate the optical features of Pt,Pd,N-CDs. The appeared peaks at 254 and 365 nm in the UV-Vis spectrum ( Fig. 3) pertain to the π–π* and n–π* transitions, respectively. The photograph of Pt,Pd,N-CDs solution under a 365 nm UV light irradiation was given in inset to Fig. 3b, exhibiting a bright blue luminescence. As shown in Fig. S3, the synthesized CDs demonstrate an excitation-dependent luminescence feature. The highest fluorescence emission at 467 nm is acquired with an excitation wavelength of 380 nm (Fig. S4). The quantum yield of Pt,Pd,N-CDs was computed to be 58% utilizing quinine sulfate as a reference solution (QY=0.54).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The role of Pt,Pd,N-CDs in the chemiluminescence reaction
ce:section.ce:para: The performance of the Pt,Pd,N-CDs on the CH3CN−H2O2 CL reaction in the basic conditions was examined and compared with corresponding single metal ion and N-doped CDs. As can be seen in Fig. 4, among the synthesized CDs, Pt,Pd,N-CDs have the highest enhancing effect on the CL signal. Therefore, Pt,Pd,N-CDs were used as the enhancer in the subsequent measurements. The CL spectrum of CH3CN−H2O2 system was recorded by a spectrofluorimeter in continuous mode to recognize the fluorophore species in the reaction. The CL spectrum ( Fig. 5) exhibits an emission band between 350–650 nm, with a maximum wavelength at around ∼490 nm. The obtained CL spectrum is similar to the Pt,Pd,N-CDs FL spectrum with the excitation wavelength of 440 nm, which can be inferred that the fluorophore species in the CL reaction are Pt,Pd,N-CDs. To further investigate the mechanism of CL generation, the effect of different radical acceptors was assessed on the CL signal. The considerable decrease in CL intensity in the presence of these radical receptors indicates the involvement of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in the CL reaction ( Table 1). Utilizing the obtained finding, the potential mechanism of the CL reaction is given below:

As mentioned in the literature [31–33], singlet oxygens (1O2) can be formed in the reaction between hydrogen peroxide and nitrile-containing compounds in an alkaline solution (Reaction 1). The combination of singlet oxygen molecules leads to the formation of (1O2)2* (Reaction 2) [33], which then act as energy donors and transfer their energy to the Pt,Pd,N-CDs as acceptors (Reaction 3). Eventually, the CL emission is generated by returning the excited Pt,Pd,N-CDs* to the ground state.
(1) CH3CN + 2H2O2→1O2 + CH3CONH2 + H2O
(2)
1O2 + 1O2→(1O2)2*
(3) (1O2)2* + Pt,Pd,N-CDs→Pt,Pd,N-CDs* + 2O2
(4) Pt,Pd,N-CDs*→Pt,Pd,N-CDs + hν (∼ 490 nm)

Hydrogen peroxide is a weak acid and deprotonated to hydroperoxyl anion (HO2 −) in alkaline conditions (Reaction 5) [34,35]. hydroperoxyl anion is unstable in aqueous mediums and dissociates to the ROS (Reaction 6) [34]. Based on the peroxidase-like behavior of metal ion-doped CDs, Pt,Pd,N-CDs can act as catalysts in this step (Reaction 6), and facilitate the dissociation of hydrogen peroxide into ROS. Then, a chain of reactions is initiated by the produced ROS (•O2 − and •OH) [36–40], which finally lead to the formation of singlet oxygens (Reactions 7–12). Therefore, Pt,Pd,N-CDs act both as fluorophores and catalysts by hastening the production of ROS.
(5) H2O2 + OH−→HO2 − + H2O
(6)
H
2
O
2
+
HO
2
−
⟶
Pt , Pd , N − CDs
O
2
−
⋅
+
O
⋅
H +
H
2
O
(7)
•O2 − + H2O2→•OH + OH− + O2
(8) 2•O2 − + 2H2O→1O2 + H2O2 + 2 OH−
(9)
•O2 − + •OH→1O2 + OH−
(10)
•OH + •OH→1/2 (1O2) + H2O
(11)
•OH + H2O2→•HO2 + H2O
(12)
•HO2 + •HO2→1O2 + H2O2

Besides, Pt,Pd,N-CDs•+ and Pt,Pd,N-CDs•- can be produced by hole and electron injection from •OH and •O2 - respectively. Then, as a result of electron-hole pair recombination, Pt,Pd,N-CDs* are obtained, which decompose to Pt,Pd,N-CDs with the emission of light at about ∼ 490 nm.

Different kinds of doped CDs (Pt,N-CDs and Pd,N-CDs) for further investigation of catalytic performance were synthesized in this research. As depicted in Fig. 4, Pt,Pd,N- CDs show a higher increasing impact than other CDs. To elucidate this matter, first, the quantum yields of synthesized CDs were calculated. The quantum yields of all doped CDs are almost in the same range (at about 63–58%), so their different enhancement effects cannot be due to the difference in their fluorescence behavior. Then, the effect of ascorbic acid (2 mM) as a radical acceptor was assessed on the CL intensity of the CH3CN-H2O2 reaction in the presence of the synthesized CDs. As results show (Table S1), in the presence of Pt,Pd,N-CDs the quenching percentage is higher than the other synthesized CDs. Therefore, it can be concluded that in the presence of Pt,Pd,N-CDs, more free radicals are produced, which leads to a greater increase of CL intensity compared to other CDs doped with only one metal ion. Therefore, the better catalytic performance of Pt,Pd,N-CDs presumably arises from the synergetic effect of Pt and Pd ions doped to CDs.

To gain the maximum amplification effect of Pt,Pd,N-CDs, the influence of variables including the concentration of H2O2, CH3CN, NaOH, and amount of the Pt,Pd,N-CDs were studied on the CL signal of the CH3CN-H2O2 reaction. Based on the obtained results (Figs. S5–S8) 40 (v/v%) of CH3CN, 0.225 M of H2O2, 0.2 M of NaOH, and 100 µL of Pt,Pd,N-CDs were chosen as optimal values.

We observed that upon the addition of Fe(II), the intensity of the CL system was gradually increased ( Fig. 6). Thus, a sensitive CL probe was designed for the determination Fe(II). The CL signal exhibits a linear response to the logarithm of Fe(II) concentration in the range of 0.002–8.0 μM (Fig. 6). The related linear data was expressed as I = 977,901logC+681,109, R2=0.999, where I is the CL intensity, and C is the concentration of Fe(II) in nM. The detection limit of this method was computed to be 1.0 nM based on 3 sy/x/m (where sy/x is the standard deviation of residuals and m is the slope of calibration graph), and the RSD was 2.9% for five replicate determinations of 5.0 μM Fe(II).

The mechanism of Fe(II)-enhanced CL signal can be attributed to the Fenton reaction between Fe(II) and H2O2. H2O2 is decomposed to a hydroxyl radical and a hydroxide ion by Fe(II) based on the following reaction:
(13) Fe2+ + H2O2→Fe3+ + OH- + •OH
Therefore, Fenton reaction leads to an increase in the amount of ROS, which boosted the CL signal of the system.
The comparison studies (Table S2) of the analytical methods demonstrate that our proposed method has an appropriate linear range and better sensitivity than other reported strategies for Fe(II) ions.

The impact of various inorganic ions as potentially interfering species in the Fe(II) assay was investigated under optimized conditions. The results of this survey are summarized in Table S3. The tolerated limit of each ion is taken as a relative error not larger than 5%. Pursuant to the consequences, the proposed CL method has an appropriate selectivity for Fe(II) assay.

The offered CL probe was utilized for the analysis of real samples. We used this method for the analysis of spiked water samples. Table 2 displays that the recovery percentages are between 96% and 105%, which expresses the reliability and accuracy of the method for the analysis of water samples. We then validated the probe by analyzing a certified reference material (Oyster Tissue, SRM 1566b). There is a good agreement for iron amount between the reported value and our obtained result ( Table 3). Also, the student t-test implies that there is no significant difference between the two values, which further affirms the accuracy of the method. The Student t-values were calculated by the following equation:
t =
(
x
̅
−
μ
0
)
s
/
n
where
x
̅
is the mean of three determinations, μ0 represents the added amount, s is the standard deviation of measurements and n is the number of determinations for each sample.

In conclusion, we have shown the application of Pt,Pd,N-CDs on the CH3CN–H2O2 CL reaction in alkaline condition. We demonstrated that the CL originate from the Pt,Pd,N-CDs, which form by the energy transfer from (1O2)2* as well as by electron-hole pair recombination between Pt,Pd,N-CDs•- and Pt,Pd,N-CDs•+. Furthermore, the Pt,Pd,N-CDs have a catalytic behavior and boost the CL intensity by the increment of the production of ROS. It was seen that the intensity of CL increases in the presence of Fe(II), which is caused by the Fenton reaction between Fe(II) and H2O2. Our results verify the promising potential of the offered CL reaction for the monitoring of Fe(II).
Elnaz Delnavaz: Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Mohammad Amjadi: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.synthmet.2024.117604.
